explanation_md: `volume()` is `const` so it can be called through the `const Box&` parameter. First call: 3³ = 27. Second call: 4³ = 64. The `const` member function reads `side` but does not modify it.

options.a: The `Score` class needs a constructor to compile.
options.b: `getValue()` is not marked `const`, so it cannot be called on the `const Score&` parameter inside `display`.
options.c: `display` should take `Score` by value, not by `const` reference.

explanation_md: When `display` receives a `const Score&`, the compiler only allows calls to member functions marked `const`. `getValue()` reads `value` but is not marked `const`, so the compiler rejects the call. Fix: `int getValue() const { return value; }`.

prompt_md: Which of the following statements about `const` member functions is correct?

options.a: A `const` member function can modify data members as long as they are `public`.
options.b: A `const` member function can call non-`const` member functions of the same class.
options.c: A `const` member function cannot modify any data member of the object.
options.d: Only static member functions can be marked `const`.

explanation_md: Inside a `const` member function, the implicit object is treated as `const`, so any write to a data member is a compile error. Access specifiers (`public`/`private`) are irrelevant here. A `const` function cannot call non-`const` functions because they might modify the object.

`isZero()` returns `true` (printed as `1`) when `count == 0`. After `increment()`, `count` is 1, so `isZero()` returns `false` (printed as `0`). `isZero()` is `const` because it only reads `count`; `increment()` is not `const` because it modifies `count`.

prompt_md: Why should getter functions (functions that just return a member value) always be marked `const`?

options.a: Because `const` makes the function run faster.
options.b: Because without `const`, the getter cannot be inlined by the compiler.
options.c: Because without `const`, the getter cannot be called on `const` objects or through `const` references, limiting where the class can be used.
options.d: Because `const` is required for functions that return a value by copy.

explanation_md: Marking a getter `const` is about correctness and usability, not performance. If a getter is not `const`, it cannot be called when the object is `const` or passed as a `const` reference — a very common pattern. This severely limits where your class can be used.
